answers.field_label: Describe What Work Needs To Be Done
answers.value:
Our primary need is to address significant deterioration in the church roof and related water damage to the sanctuary and education wing. The existing asphalt shingle roof was installed in 1997 and has exceeded its expected lifespan. Over the past three years we have experienced increasing leaks, particularly over the choir loft, the back of the sanctuary, and in two classrooms in the education wing. During heavy rains, water has been observed dripping from three separate locations in the sanctuary ceiling, resulting in water stains on the plaster, peeling paint, and damage to several of the original wooden pews.

The proposed scope of work includes:

1. Complete removal of the existing asphalt shingle roofing on the sanctuary, education wing, and fellowship hall, including underlayment where it has been compromised.
2. Replacement of any rotten or weakened roof decking, especially around the eaves and near the bell tower where soft spots have been identified by our volunteer maintenance team.
3. Installation of new underlayment, ice and water shield in vulnerable areas (valleys, eaves, and around the chimney), and high-quality architectural shingles rated for at least 30 years.
4. Replacement and proper flashing of all roof penetrations, including the HVAC vents, the brick chimney behind the pulpit wall, and the base of the small bell tower.
5. Repair and repainting of interior ceiling areas where water damage has occurred, particularly in the sanctuary above the rear pews and along the right-side aisle, as well as two Sunday school classrooms and the hallway ceiling outside the nursery.
6. Inspection and repair of the gutter and downspout system so that water is carried away from the foundation. At present, several downspouts are missing or damaged, and we have noted pooling water near the southwest corner of the building after storms.

A secondary, but related, need is to address the damage that has already occurred to interior finishes and one section of hardwood flooring. The hardwood floor at the back of the sanctuary, near the entrance doors, has begun to cup and darken due to repeated exposure to moisture. We hope that as part of this project we can have that section of flooring repaired or replaced and the area refinished to match the rest of the sanctuary floor.

Our building is the original 1928 sanctuary with a small education wing added in the 1960s. Because of the age of the structure and the prominence of the church in our small community, we are trying to preserve the historic character of the building while ensuring it is safe, dry, and functional. The leaks have already forced us to rope off a few pews during heavy rains and to place buckets in the aisle, which is both embarrassing for visitors and potentially hazardous for our older members who use walkers or canes.

In summary, the work that needs to be done is a full roof replacement on all connected structures, associated structural repair to any compromised decking, restoration of damaged interior ceilings and walls, repair of water-damaged hardwood flooring, and repair or replacement of gutters and downspouts to prevent further water intrusion and protect the foundation.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Has The Church Had Any Professional Evaluation Of The Needed Work Or A Professional Cost Estimate Done
answers.value:
Yes. In September 2025, the Board of Trustees invited three local roofing contractors to inspect the building and provide written estimates. Two of the companies—High Plains Roofing of Big Spring and Covenant Roofing & Construction of Midland—submitted detailed bids. Both contractors agreed that the current roof has reached the end of its serviceable life and that patching individual leak points would no longer be cost-effective.

High Plains Roofing estimated a total project cost of $46,800 for complete tear-off, replacement of damaged decking, installation of new underlayment and 30-year architectural shingles, and basic repair of flashing around the chimney and vents. This estimate did not include any interior ceiling or flooring repair.

Covenant Roofing & Construction provided a more comprehensive estimate totaling $58,400. Their proposal includes:
- Full tear-off and replacement of roofing and underlayment on the sanctuary, education wing, and fellowship hall (approximately 6,200 square feet of roof area).
- Replacement of any rotten decking at $3.25 per square foot, with an allowance of up to 400 square feet included in the base estimate.
- New ice and water shield in valleys, along eaves, and around penetrations.
- Replacement of all flashing and installation of new metal drip edge.
- Installation of continuous ridge venting to improve ventilation in the attic spaces.
- Replacement and reconfiguration of the gutter system on the south and west sides of the building, including four new downspouts and two extended drain lines to direct water away from the foundation.
- Interior repairs: patching, re-texturing, and repainting damaged ceiling areas in the sanctuary and education wing; and repair or replacement of up to 120 square feet of hardwood flooring at the rear of the sanctuary.

In addition to these roofing estimates, one of our trustees, who is a retired general contractor, conducted an informal assessment of the interior damage and concurred that the ceiling plaster and a section of the hardwood floor would need professional repair once the roof leaks are fully resolved.

We are attaching copies of the written estimates from both roofing companies, including their itemized breakdowns and proof of insurance, with this application. Both companies are licensed and insured, and each has experience working on older church buildings in our region. Based on these evaluations, the Board of Trustees has determined that the more comprehensive proposal from Covenant Roofing & Construction best addresses both the immediate need to stop the leaks and the associated interior damage that has already occurred.

answers.field_label: What Is The Churchs Operating Budget For The Current Calendar Year
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Briefly Tell Why The Congregation Cannot Undertake This Project By Itself
answers.value:
Our congregation is small and aging, and while we are faithful in our giving, our financial capacity is limited. For the current calendar year, our total operating budget is $128,500. Of that amount, approximately 72% is dedicated to fixed expenses such as pastoral salary and benefits, utilities, insurance, and basic building maintenance (HVAC servicing, lawn care, custodial supplies). Another 15% supports our ongoing ministries and outreach programs, including our weekly community food pantry, a partnership with the local elementary school for weekend backpack meals, and modest support for conference missions.

Over the past five years, our average Sunday attendance has declined as several long-time members have passed away or moved to be closer to family. Our current average attendance of around 52 people includes a significant number of retired individuals on fixed incomes. While they are generous, they do not have the capacity to dramatically increase their giving in the short term without jeopardizing their own financial stability.

In 2023, we also faced unexpected expenses when our 30-year-old HVAC unit serving the fellowship hall failed in July during a period of extreme heat. We were forced to replace the unit at a cost of $11,200, which we covered through a combination of emergency reserve funds and a small internal loan from our memorial fund. As a result, our reserve and memorial accounts are now at the lowest levels they have been in more than a decade, and the Trustees have agreed that we must maintain a minimum reserve to cover insurance deductibles and future emergencies.

We have attempted to address the roof issues with temporary measures over the last two years. Volunteers have patched obvious problem spots and re-sealed some flashing, and we have paid for several small repair visits from a local roofer, totaling about $3,400. These efforts have slowed, but not stopped, the leaks. The professional evaluations we received this fall make it clear that a full roof replacement is now necessary, along with associated repairs to interior ceilings and flooring. The lowest comprehensive bid we received for the full scope of work is $58,400—an amount that is simply beyond what we can absorb within our current budget or realistically raise in a short period.

If we attempted to take on the full cost ourselves, we would either have to drastically cut our ministry and mission commitments, defer other essential maintenance (such as replacing aging electrical panels and addressing accessibility improvements), or take on debt that we are not confident we could service responsibly. Our finance committee has run multiple scenarios, and even if every active household increased their giving by 10%, we would still fall significantly short of the funds needed within the next 12–18 months.

For these reasons—our limited and declining giving base, recent unexpected expenses, already depleted reserves, and the size and urgency of the needed repairs—we are not able to undertake this project entirely on our own and are seeking assistance from the West District to help preserve our building and continue our ministry in this community.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Does The Congregation Have Any Funds To Devote To This Project
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Have Future Fundraising Activities Been Planned
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
As of January 2026, the congregation has set aside $9,750 specifically for building repairs, of which the Trustees have designated $7,500 as the initial contribution toward the roof project. These funds come from a combination of prior-year budget surplus ($3,200), memorial gifts ($2,800), and proceeds from a 2024 Christmas bazaar and bake sale ($3,750). We are intentionally leaving a small balance in the general building reserve for emergencies, but we are prepared to commit the full $7,500 to this project as soon as construction is scheduled.

In addition to existing funds, we have begun planning a series of fundraising activities for 2026 to help contribute further to the project costs:

1. "Raise the Roof" Capital Campaign: Beginning March 1, 2026, we will launch a six-month capital campaign with a goal of raising an additional $15,000 from within the congregation and through appeals to former members and friends who have moved away but still feel connected to the church. We have already drafted a letter explaining the situation, including photos of the water damage, and will mail and email it widely. Church members will be invited to make one-time gifts or six-month pledges specifically dedicated to the roof fund.

2. Community Barbecue and Hymn Sing: For early May 2026, we are planning an outdoor barbecue supper and hymn sing on the church grounds. Several local musicians have agreed to donate their time, and one of our members, who runs a small catering business, has offered to prepare the food at a reduced cost. We will sell meal tickets in advance and at the door, and we hope to net at least $3,000 after expenses. We will also set up a donation table with information about the roof project for guests who wish to contribute above the ticket price.

3. Online Giving and Social Media Outreach: Our younger members have agreed to help set up an online giving option specifically for the roof project through our existing church website and social media accounts. They plan to share short video clips explaining why the building matters to the community—for example, testimony from a couple who were married in the sanctuary 40 years ago and from a local teacher whose students receive weekend food bags prepared in our fellowship hall. We do not have a specific dollar goal for online giving yet, but we hope this will both raise awareness and bring in gifts from former members and friends who live out of town.

4. Craft and Quilt Sale: Several of our older members are avid quilters and crafters. They have already begun working on quilts, table runners, and handmade items that will be sold in the fall of 2026, with all proceeds dedicated to the building fund. In past years, similar efforts have raised between $1,500 and $2,000.

While we know these activities will not cover the full cost of the project, they demonstrate our commitment to contributing as much as we reasonably can. We are not asking the West District to fund 100% of the repair, but rather to partner with us so that our combined resources—our designated funds on hand, planned fundraising, and district assistance—will be sufficient to complete the work promptly before further damage occurs.
